theme: The moment 200 people almost ate dinner in the rain — and what saved it

linkedin.text:
At 14:00, a thunderstorm was about to ruin a €120k dinner for 200 executives. They never found out.

Tuscany, October 2025. A hilltop vineyard dinner planned for 19:30. The day-of forecast flipped: 90% storms by 18:30.

Most agencies panic, refund, or dump everyone into a hotel buffet — exactly what the client paid to avoid.

What we did instead, in 4 hours:
14:15 — Locked a 220-seat barrel cellar with the vineyard owner
14:30 — Three parallel calls: catering flow, beam-rigged lighting, 22 tables by 16:30
14:45 — All confirmed. We committed. We didn't tell the client — only if it worked
15:00-18:00 — Full teardown, candles in, quartet repositioned 4 times for acoustics
18:30 — Storm hit, exactly on schedule
19:30 — 200 executives walked into a candlelit cellar that looked designed for them three months ago

At the debrief the CEO said: "That cellar IS the memory of the trip."

That's what production means: three plans ready, switched invisibly. Weather is the easy one — try a chef who quits day-of, or a speaker stuck at the airport. We've handled all of it, so your CEO never has to.

💬 DM us "PLAYBOOK" to see how we build contingency into every event.

#EventProduction #IncentiveTravel #CorporateEvents #B2BEvents #MICE #Leadership

facebook.text:
Picture this: 200 of your top executives, a hilltop dinner you've planned for months, and at 14:00 the sky turns. Storm by 18:30. Dinner at 19:30. ⛈️

Most agencies would send the panic email. We made three phone calls.

In four hours we moved the entire evening into a 16th-century barrel cellar — candles, tables, a string quartet repositioned until the acoustics were right.

At 19:30 the guests walked in and never knew there had been a Plan B. The CEO told us later: "That cellar IS the memory of the trip."

That's the difference between coordinating an event and producing one.

Ever had a plan saved at the last minute? Tell us in the comments. 💬
